title: Machine-Generated Email Identifiers with Rapid Account Creation and Low-Value Testing
narrative: Fraudsters deploy automated scripts to generate throwaway email accounts using random alphanumeric strings (e.g., x7k9m2qw@gmail.com, p4n8v1zr@yahoo.com) for card testing and low-value purchases. These emails lack human naming patternsâ€”no words, names, or recognizable sequences. A typical burst involves 15-30 accounts created within 90 minutes, each attempting $3.99-$9.99 transactions on digital goods (ProductCD='W'). The P_emaildomain is often freemail (gmail, yahoo, outlook), while R_emaildomain remains null or mismatched. Transactions cluster in late-night hours (txn_hour 1-5 AM), suggesting automated execution. Email entropy exceeds 3.8 bits per character, and card1_txn_count_24hr stays at 1-2 per card to evade velocity triggers. Successful tests escalate to $200-$500 purchases within 6 hours using the same card/email pair. AVS and CVV responses initially match to establish trust before bust-out phase.
indicators: P_emaildomain local part entropy >3.8 bits/char with no dictionary words or common name patterns
15-30 unique P_emaildomain accounts created within 90-minute window sharing addr1 or device fingerprint
Initial TransactionAmt $3.99-$9.99 on ProductCD='W', followed by $200-$500 charges within 6 hours on same card
78% of transactions occur txn_hour 1-5 AM with is_night=1
R_emaildomain null or mismatched to P_emaildomain in 85%+ of transactions
card1_txn_count_24hr remains â‰¤2 per card despite multiple email variations
AVS_match=Y and CVV_match=M in first 1-3 test transactions, then declines or partial matches in escalation phase
summary: Automated scripts create 15-30 accounts with random-string emails (high entropy, no real names) within 90 minutes, attempting small purchases ($4-$10) on digital goods during late-night hours, then rapidly escalating to larger charges using the same cards.
reasoning: Fraudsters test stolen card validity with low-dollar transactions that fly under fraud thresholds, then immediately exploit confirmed cards for high-value purchases before detection—throwaway emails and tight timing prevent victim notification and maximize theft window.
indicator_explanations: Email address looks randomly generated with no recognizable human naming patterns
Dozens of new email accounts created in rapid succession from same address or device
Small test purchases followed by sudden jump to much larger amounts on same card
Activity concentrated in middle-of-night hours when fraud monitoring is weakest
Recipient email missing or doesn't match purchaser in vast majority of transactions
Each card used only once or twice despite churning through many email accounts
Initial transactions pass verification checks then subsequent attempts start failing or degrading
